narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.30 and LSAT score of 28—both notably below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students in our dataset. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 96% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high failure probability means the model saw your profile as carrying substantial risk compared to students who typically succeed. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as stronger late-stage performance is a key indicator for most who pass. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, again in the lower ranks far below the average of about 5.7, reinforcing the model's concern over your early academic standing relative to successful students. Your LSAT score also played a significant role, with its lower-than-average value further tipping the scales toward a failure prediction rather than success. While factors like your full-time enrollment aligned with the vast majority of students (over 92%), they weren't enough to offset these academic red flags. In essence, your grades in the later undergraduate years mattered most here, as they deviated sharply from the patterns of bar passers, who usually rank in the middle or top of their classes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances overcome these hurdles—but the model prioritizes these metrics based on historical outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your Year 1 and especially Year 3 deciles through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could significantly shift your predicted odds, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Keep pushing; your determination as a Black man from a middle-income background positions you well to defy these statistical odds with targeted improvements.
